_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Our new hiring test predicts job success: 80% of employees who scored high on it later received strong performance reviews.
items.Stem: Which of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the argument?
items.ChoiceA: How much it costs the company to administer the test.
items.ChoiceB: What percentage of employees who scored low on the test also received strong reviews.
items.ChoiceC: Whether a rival firm's hiring test predicts job success more accurately than this one.
items.ChoiceD: Whether employees found the hiring test enjoyable.
items.ChoiceE: How long the hiring test takes an applicant to complete.
items.WhyWrongA: Cost is irrelevant to whether the test predicts success.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Without the low-scorers' base rate, the 80% figure cannot show the test predicts anything.
items.WhyWrongC: How a rival's test performs is a separate comparison that does not test this argument.
items.WhyWrongE: Test length does not bear on predictive validity.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The city claims its anti-litter campaign worked: litter complaints dropped 40% after the campaign launched.
items.Stem: The answer to which of the following questions would be most relevant to evaluating the claim?
items.ChoiceA: What slogan did the campaign use in its advertisements?
items.ChoiceB: How much money did the anti-litter campaign cost to run?
items.ChoiceC: Did any other cities run similar anti-litter campaigns?
items.ChoiceD: How many residents said they remembered seeing the campaign?
items.ChoiceE: Did the city reduce the number of ways residents could report litter during that period?
items.WhyWrongA: The slogan is irrelevant to the drop's meaning.
items.WhyWrongB: Cost is irrelevant to whether litter actually fell.
items.WhyWrongC: Other cities' campaigns do not bear on this city's result.
items.WhyWrongD: Recall is weakly related but does not test whether complaints track litter.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. If reporting channels shrank, fewer complaints may not mean less litter.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A manager argues that new software sped up order processing, since average processing time dropped after it was installed.
items.Stem: Which of the following would be most useful to determine in order to evaluate the manager's argument?
items.ChoiceA: Whether a competing software package processes orders faster than this one.
items.ChoiceB: Whether the volume or complexity of orders changed when the software was installed.
items.ChoiceC: Whether employees found the new software easy to use.
items.ChoiceD: Which vendor supplied the new software.
items.ChoiceE: How much the software's annual license costs.
items.WhyWrongA: How a competing package performs is a separate comparison, not a test of this claim.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. If orders got simpler or fewer, that — not the software — could explain the faster times.
items.WhyWrongC: Ease of use does not establish the cause of the time drop.
items.WhyWrongE: License cost is irrelevant to processing speed.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Researchers found that people who drink coffee daily live longer on average than people who do not. They conclude that drinking coffee extends life.
items.Stem: The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful in evaluating the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Whether daily coffee drinkers differ from non-drinkers in other habits, such as smoking or exercise, that affect longevity.
items.ChoiceB: Whether daily tea drinkers also live longer than people who drink no tea.
items.ChoiceC: How much caffeine a typical cup of coffee contains.
items.ChoiceD: Whether coffee is a more popular beverage than tea worldwide.
items.ChoiceE: How the researchers measured each participant's daily coffee intake.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If coffee drinkers differ in other health habits, those habits, not coffee, might explain the longer lives.
items.WhyWrongB: Tea drinkers are a separate group and do not test this argument.
items.WhyWrongC: Caffeine content does not bear on whether coffee itself extends life.
items.WhyWrongD: Global popularity is irrelevant to longevity.
items.WhyWrongE: How intake was measured matters only modestly and does not address the causal gap.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] After the town installed brighter streetlights, nighttime traffic accidents fell by 30%. Officials conclude that the brighter lights made the roads safer.
items.Stem: Which of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the officials' conclusion?
items.ChoiceA: Whether the brighter lights consume more electricity than the old ones.
items.ChoiceB: How much the new streetlights cost the town to install.
items.ChoiceC: What color of light the new streetlights emit.
items.ChoiceD: Whether daytime traffic accidents also fell over the same period.
items.ChoiceE: Whether the volume of nighttime traffic changed after the brighter lights were installed.
items.WhyWrongA: Electricity use is irrelevant to accident rates.
items.WhyWrongB: Installation cost does not bear on safety.
items.WhyWrongC: Light color is irrelevant to the argument.
items.WhyWrongD: A daytime drop would hint at another cause, but this is less decisive than the traffic-volume question.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. If far fewer cars drove at night, the drop in accidents could be due to less traffic, not the lights.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A supplement maker notes that athletes who take its protein powder gain more muscle than athletes who do not, and concludes that the powder builds muscle.
items.Stem: The answer to which of the following questions would be most relevant to evaluating the conclusion?
items.ChoiceA: Whether muscle gains were measured the same way in both groups.
items.ChoiceB: Whether the powder costs more per serving than the supplements rival athletes use.
items.ChoiceC: Whether the athletes who take the powder also follow more intense training regimens than those who do not.
items.ChoiceD: How many years the company has been marketing this particular protein powder.
items.ChoiceE: Whether the powder's flavor is one that most competitive athletes find palatable.
items.WhyWrongA: Consistent measurement matters somewhat but does not address the training confound.
items.WhyWrongB: Price relative to rivals does not test the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. If powder users train harder, their extra muscle may come from training rather than the powder.
items.WhyWrongD: How long the product has sold is irrelevant.
items.WhyWrongE: Flavor has no bearing on muscle growth.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A school introduced free breakfast and saw test scores rise the following year. The principal concludes that the free breakfast improved students' academic performance.
items.Stem: Which of the following would be most useful to determine in evaluating the principal's conclusion?
items.ChoiceA: Whether students at nearby schools without free breakfast also improved that year.
items.ChoiceB: How early in the morning breakfast was served.
items.ChoiceC: How much the free-breakfast program costs per student.
items.ChoiceD: Which specific foods were served at breakfast.
items.ChoiceE: Whether the school made other changes, such as adopting a new curriculum, during the same year.
items.WhyWrongA: A rise at comparison schools would hint at another cause but is less direct than the same-school changes.
items.WhyWrongB: The exact serving time is irrelevant.
items.WhyWrongC: Program cost does not bear on whether breakfast raised scores.
items.WhyWrongD: The particular menu is irrelevant to the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. If the school changed other things at the same time, those changes could explain the higher scores.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A pundit argues that the new mayor caused the city's economic boom, since the local economy grew rapidly after she took office.
items.Stem: The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful in evaluating the pundit's argument?
items.ChoiceA: Whether the surrounding region's economy grew at a similar rate over the same period.
items.ChoiceB: How many years the mayor's term lasts.
items.ChoiceC: By what margin the mayor won her election.
items.ChoiceD: Whether the mayor had prior experience in economic policy.
items.ChoiceE: Which political party the mayor belongs to.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If the whole region boomed, a broader trend, not the mayor, may have driven the city's growth.
items.WhyWrongB: Term length is irrelevant to the growth's cause.
items.WhyWrongC: Her margin of victory does not bear on the economic cause.
items.WhyWrongD: Her background is only weakly relevant and does not test the causal link.
items.WhyWrongE: Party affiliation is irrelevant to the argument.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A report finds that students who use the school's new tutoring app score higher on exams than those who do not, so the school concludes the app raises scores.
items.Stem: Which of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the school's conclusion?
items.ChoiceB: Whether the students who chose to use the app were already higher-achieving before they began using it.
items.ChoiceC: Whether the app is available in more than one language.
items.ChoiceD: Whether students who used the app spent similar amounts of time on it.
items.ChoiceE: How much storage space the app requires on a student's phone.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. If stronger students self-selected into using the app, their higher scores may reflect prior ability, not the app.
items.WhyWrongC: Language availability does not test the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongD: Usage time matters somewhat but does not address the self-selection problem.
items.WhyWrongE: Storage requirements are irrelevant to exam scores.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A city added a protected bike lane, and bicycle commuting rose 20%. Planners conclude that the bike lane caused more people to bike to work.
items.Stem: The answer to which of the following questions would be most relevant to evaluating the planners' conclusion?
items.ChoiceA: Whether other factors that encourage biking, such as rising fuel prices or a new bike-share program, changed at the same time.
items.ChoiceB: How many feet wider the new protected bike lane is than the old painted one.
items.ChoiceC: Whether bicycle commuting also rose in nearby cities that added no bike lanes.
items.ChoiceD: What color the city chose for the new bike-lane markings and signs.
items.ChoiceE: How much it cost the city to paint the bike lane.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If fuel prices or a bike-share program changed too, those factors, not the lane, might explain the rise.
items.WhyWrongB: The lane's width does not bear on the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongC: A rise elsewhere would hint at a broader trend but is less direct than the same-city factors.
items.WhyWrongE: Construction cost is irrelevant to why commuting rose.
